metadata.match_reason: Internal teams use the exact stack: LangGraph, RAG, Milvus, FastAPI, Azure SQL for customer-support chatbot. Active SE III postings with GenAI/LLM/Agentic AI requirements.
metadata.notes: Stack match is unusually exact (Milvus + FastAPI + RAG + LangGraph in production). Bengaluru-only role.

research_summary: Walmart Global Tech India (10K+ employees, Bengaluru) is hiring SE-III GenAI engineers to build agentic AI and RAG systems. CTO Suresh Kumar published 'All in on Agents' (Jul 2025) announcing 4 super agents. The tech stack -- LangGraph, Milvus, FastAPI, RAG -- is an exact match with Amit's production work. Internal engineers like Urvashi Ramdasani and Jigyasa Kodnani are already building LangGraph + RAG systems at Walmart. Email pattern: firstname.lastname@walmart.com. 9 contacts across Tier 1 (senior ML), Tier 3 (mid ML), and Tier 4 (recruiters).
jd_full_text:
Software Engineer III -- ML Engineer (GenAI) | Walmart Global Tech India | Bengaluru

Core requirements (synthesized from LinkedIn, Instahyre, and public job data):

- Agentic AI and LangGraph: Build multi-agent systems with conditional routing, state management, reflection loops, and tool-calling for reasoning tasks
- Enterprise RAG: Design production-grade RAG pipelines -- custom chunking strategies, semantic search, hybrid retrieval (keyword + vector search), embedding model selection and evaluation (sentence transformers)
- Vector Databases (Milvus): High-scale vector store expertise -- indexing strategies, query scalability, integration with 1M+ enterprise records
- Engineering and MLOps: Production Python, Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, LangSmith observability for agentic workflow tracing
- Core ML: B.S./M.S. in CS or Engineering, ML algorithms, data structures, LLM fine-tuning (PEFT/LoRA)
- 3+ years software engineering with exposure to ranking, recommendation, or ML systems at scale
- FastAPI or equivalent backend experience
- Azure cloud services

Context: Walmart has deployed 4 AI super agents (Sparky for customers, associate, supplier, developer). Internal teams are solving 'Agent and RAG Sprawl' at enterprise scale. The Bengaluru IDC is a core engineering hub.
tailoring_notes: Summary rewritten to lead with agentic AI + enterprise RAG + Milvus -- JD explicitly calls out LangGraph, Milvus, hybrid retrieval. Skills reordered: LLMs and Techniques first (Agentic AI, LangGraph per JD first requirement), Vector DBs second (Milvus called out by name in JD), Languages 3rd, Infrastructure 4th; Hardware/IoT dropped. IIT Delhi trimmed to 1 bullet. Projects: WeepScope replaced with SAP Analytics Bot.

contacts.research_hook: Staff MLE at Walmart during the 'All in on Agents' push (CTO Suresh Kumar, Jul 2025) -- likely building or scaling the ML infra behind Walmart's 4 super agents. The 'Why solving Agent and RAG Sprawl is essential' blog (Apr 2025) shows the exact production challenge he is working on.
contacts.subject: Walmart's RAG sprawl post -- built something similar
contacts.content:
Hi Prakhar,

Came across Walmart Global Tech's "Why solving Agent and RAG Sprawl is essential" post a few weeks ago. The core problem -- managing many agents and RAG pipelines without them drifting or stepping on each other -- is something I have been working through for the past year.

At Vivansh I built a permission-aware RAG chatbot on LangGraph where 250+ per-user access controls had to be enforced in real time across 10,000+ chunks. Migrated from FAISS to Milvus after benchmarking both on production data, and got MRR from 45% to 95% on 50K+ queries/month. The hardest part was not the retrieval -- it was keeping agent state coherent across tool calls when permissions varied per query.

If there is a role on your team for someone with this kind of production background, I would appreciate 15 minutes. Open to a call next Tuesday or Thursday.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | github.com/amit-gohel
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: Walmart's RAG sprawl post -- built something similar
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Prakhar,

Bumping this in case it got buried. Since I sent the last note, I put together a short writeup on the permission index approach -- a separate Milvus collection used only for access control, cross-referenced before every retrieval call. Happy to share it if useful.

Amit
amitgohel.dev
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: Walmart's RAG sprawl post -- built something similar
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Hi Prakhar,

Last note from me. If the timing is not right or there are no open roles on the ML side, totally understood -- would still appreciate being on your radar for when that changes.

Amit
amitgohel.dev

contacts.research_hook: Staff SE in Applied AI at Walmart -- posted 'Exciting Opportunity!!' 6 months ago (likely an ML/AI hire). As Applied AI staff engineer during Walmart's agentic pivot, directly building production AI systems. 3.5K followers, active on LinkedIn.
contacts.subject: question about Walmart's applied AI stack
contacts.content:
Hi Vishal,

Read Suresh Kumar's "All in on Agents" post when it came out -- the part about 4 unified super agents replacing fragmented AI tools was interesting. As someone building production agentic systems, I am curious how the Applied AI team approaches the orchestration layer, particularly for the associate and developer agents.

Quick context: at Vivansh I built a LangGraph agentic chatbot with a stateful memory layer in PostgreSQL and 2-stage Milvus retrieval enforcing 250+ per-user permissions in real time. FastAPI with 4 async workers, 1,000+ queries/day. Session management across tool calls was the hard part -- keeping agent context coherent when permissions changed mid-session.

If your team is hiring on the applied AI side, I would love 15 minutes to hear how you think about agent state management at Walmart's scale. Open to next Tuesday or Thursday.

Amit
github.com/amit-gohel | amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: question about Walmart's applied AI stack
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Vishal,

Bumping this in case it got lost. I saw Walmart published the WIBEY platform announcement in August -- moving from model deployment to full agent infrastructure is exactly the direction I have been building toward at Vivansh.

If there is a role, I would appreciate a pointer. If not, no worries.

Amit
amitgohel.dev

contacts.follow_up_2_content:

contacts.contact_role: AI Engineer | Ex-PayPal, ZS, Amazon | Faculty at IITs/IIMs
contacts.research_hook: Rare dual role: senior AI practitioner at Walmart AND adjunct faculty at IITs/IIMs. Backgrounds at PayPal, ZS Associates, Amazon. The IIT connection maps directly to Amit's IIT Delhi research internship. Likely involved in ML research strategy and applied AI systems.
contacts.subject: IIT Delhi + Milvus RAG -- worth 15 minutes?
contacts.content:
Hi Mahesh,

Noticed you hold a faculty role at IITs while doing AI work at Walmart -- that combination of research depth and production shipping is not common. I did a 5-month research internship at IIT Delhi (Dept. of Electrical Engineering, under Dr. Lalan Kumar) before moving into production AI roles, so that bridge between research and engineering is something I know well.

At Vivansh I built a permission-aware RAG chatbot on LangGraph handling 1,000+ queries/day, and earlier rebuilt the RAG infra at Silver Touch from FAISS to Milvus -- tested 6 embedding models on production data, settled on BGE-M3 + reranker at 95% MRR on 50K+ monthly queries. The Walmart team's "RAG Sprawl" post in April described the same scaling problem I was working through.

If there is an opening on your team for someone with this background, I would appreciate 15 minutes. Next Tuesday or Thursday works.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | github.com/amit-gohel
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: IIT Delhi + Milvus RAG -- worth 15 minutes?
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Mahesh,

Quick follow-up in case my last note got buried. Since sending it, I put together a short breakdown of the Milvus vs. FAISS vs. Qdrant eval I ran on production data -- happy to share if it is useful context.

Amit
amitgohel.dev

contacts.contact_role: SDE-III, Backend and Data | FastAPI, Spark | Agentic Systems
contacts.research_hook: Her LinkedIn summary: 'Built an autonomous agentic orchestrator from scratch (FastAPI + LangGraph), including a stateful memory layer (PostgreSQL) and async tool execution.' Nearly identical to Amit's Vivansh agentic chatbot architecture. Direct technical peer on the same stack.
contacts.subject: your LangGraph orchestrator -- ran into the same thing
contacts.content:
Hi Urvashi,

Saw your LinkedIn mention the autonomous agentic orchestrator you built on FastAPI + LangGraph with stateful memory in PostgreSQL. That is basically the same architecture I shipped at Vivansh -- LangGraph for agent state, FastAPI with 4 async workers, PostgreSQL for session memory, and Milvus for retrieval.

The hard part for me was managing tool call state across permission-gated queries -- 250+ per-user access controls checked before every retrieval step, 1,000+ queries/day. I imagine you ran into similar edge cases with async tool execution at scale.

If there is room on the team for someone with this background, I would appreciate an intro to whoever is hiring on your side. Happy to chat briefly if that is easier.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | github.com/amit-gohel

contacts.follow_up_1_content:

Bumping this in case it got buried. I recently worked through async tool execution ordering in LangGraph when tool results affect subsequent tool permissions -- happy to share that approach if it is useful.

Amit
amitgohel.dev

contacts.contact_role: Software Engineer II | ex-GenAI Samsung | Multi-Agent Systems
contacts.research_hook: Ex-Samsung GenAI, Gold Medalist HBTU. Now at Walmart building multi-agent systems with LangChain and Claude. Amit also competed in Samsung Solve for Tomorrow (top 10 among 70,000) and uses the same LangChain/LangGraph stack. Strong technical common ground.
contacts.subject: ex-Samsung + LangChain -- similar path here
contacts.content:
Hi Amartya,

Came across your profile while looking into Walmart's GenAI team -- the Samsung GenAI background caught my eye. I competed in Samsung Solve for Tomorrow as well, top 10 among 70,000+ for Voicer, a sign language to speech wearable. Different track but same program.

At Vivansh I am building with LangGraph and LangChain for a multi-tenant agentic chatbot -- permission-aware retrieval over Milvus, GPT-4o and Qwen3-Coder in the same pipeline, 1,000+ queries/day. Before that I ran the RAG infra rebuild at Silver Touch: FAISS to Milvus migration, 6 embedding models tested on production data, 45% to 95% MRR on 50K monthly queries.

If there is a team you think I should talk to at Walmart, I would appreciate the pointer. Open to a quick chat if that is easier.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | github.com/amit-gohel

contacts.subject: LangGraph Text-to-SQL + RAG -- built something similar
contacts.content:
Hi Jigyasa,

Came across your profile and noticed the LangGraph multi-agent system you built at Walmart -- Text-to-SQL plus RAG across 1M+ records. I built something adjacent at Silver Touch: an LLM-powered Text-to-SQL system querying a live SAP database, auto-generating dashboard charts from the results. Not quite 1M records, but the agent routing logic across SQL and retrieval tools was the same kind of problem.

More recently at Vivansh I took that further -- a LangGraph agentic chatbot with permission-aware Milvus retrieval enforcing 250+ access controls per query. Went from 45% to 95% MRR on 50K monthly queries at Silver Touch, now running 1,000+ queries/day at Vivansh.

If there is room on your team for someone with this kind of background, I would appreciate an intro to whoever is hiring. Happy to chat briefly if that helps.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | github.com/amit-gohel
